questions.question: Arrange the motor hierarchy from highest (top) to lowest (bottom) level:
questions.explanation: The motor hierarchy descends from: Motor Cortex (upper motor neurons, specifying what/how) → Basal Ganglia (gating initiation) → Cerebellum (error correction) → Spinal cord/brainstem (lower motor neurons, final output to muscles).

questions.question: Put the events of the readiness potential experiment in chronological order:
items: Person reports conscious urge to move
Readiness potential detected in motor cortex
correct_order: Readiness potential detected in motor cortex
Person reports conscious urge to move
questions.explanation: The sequence is: unconscious neural preparation → readiness potential appears (measurable by EEG) → conscious awareness of wanting to move (reported several seconds later) → actual movement execution.

questions.question: Order the path of a cortical motor command from brain to muscle:
items: Upper motor neurons in motor cortex fire
Lower motor neurons in spinal cord activate
correct_order: Upper motor neurons in motor cortex fire
Lower motor neurons in spinal cord activate
questions.explanation: The motor pathway: upper motor neurons fire in cortex → axons descend and cross at the medullary pyramids → synapse on lower motor neurons in the contralateral spinal cord → lower motor neurons activate muscles.

questions.question: Arrange the basal ganglia direct pathway in order from input to output:
questions.explanation: In the direct pathway: cortex excites the striatum → striatum inhibits the globus pallidus (which normally inhibits thalamus) → thalamus is released from inhibition (disinhibited) → movement is allowed to proceed.

questions.question: Order the stages of motor learning from beginner to expert:
items: Automatic performance with minimal conscious effort
correct_order: Automatic performance with minimal conscious effort
questions.explanation: Motor learning progresses: conscious effort (beginner) → broad cortical activation (early phase, recruiting many areas) → specific M1/cerebellum activation (advanced phase) → automatic performance (expert, minimal effort).

questions.question: Order these motor cortex regions from planning to execution:
questions.explanation: Movement flows from planning to execution: SMA prepares sequences and intentions → premotor cortex plans specific movements → M1 sends execution commands → lower motor neurons translate commands into muscle activation.

questions.question: Put these cerebellar error correction steps in order:
items: Motor command copy sent to cerebellum
Mismatch detected between intended and actual movement
correct_order: Motor command copy sent to cerebellum
Mismatch detected between intended and actual movement
questions.explanation: Cerebellar error correction: a copy of the motor command is sent to the cerebellum → sensory feedback about actual movement arrives → the cerebellum compares intended vs actual (detects mismatch) → corrective signals are sent to adjust the movement.

questions.question: Order the Parkinson's disease mechanism from cause to symptom:
items: Dopaminergic neurons in substantia nigra die
correct_order: Dopaminergic neurons in substantia nigra die
questions.explanation: Parkinson's mechanism: substantia nigra neurons die → dopamine levels drop in the striatum → the direct pathway (which needs dopamine to function) becomes underactive → movements are excessively inhibited, leading to tremor at rest and bradykinesia.

questions.question: Order the cat spinal transection experiment steps:
items: Hindlimbs produce coordinated walking on treadmill
Cat is placed on a moving treadmill
CPGs in spinal cord activate without brain input
correct_order: Cat is placed on a moving treadmill
CPGs in spinal cord activate without brain input
Hindlimbs produce coordinated walking on treadmill
questions.explanation: The experiment: spinal cord is cut (severing higher brain connections) → cat is placed on a treadmill → CPGs in the isolated spinal cord activate in response to treadmill movement → hindlimbs produce coordinated walking without any brain input.

questions.question: Order these population coding steps from stimulus to movement:
items: Each neuron fires according to its preferred direction
Arm moves in the population-determined direction
correct_order: Each neuron fires according to its preferred direction
Arm moves in the population-determined direction
questions.explanation: Population coding: a target is perceived → neurons in M1 fire at different rates based on their preferred directions → the vector sum ('majority vote') across the population determines the direction → the arm moves in that direction.
